name: Khazari
description:
The Khazari evolved on Drakenforge, a brutal volcanic world of crushing gravity and rivers of molten basalt, where the weak were consumed by the planet long before predators got the chance. Thick-scaled, dense-boned, and built like siege engines, the Khazari spent their early millennia not fleeing from fire but walking into it. Their silicon-laced biology thrives at temperatures that would reduce carbon-based tissue to vapour — their blood carries dissolved silicates rather than iron, and their crystalline scales shed heat the way other species shed rain. They are the galaxy's supreme metallurgists. While other species discovered smelting as a breakthrough, the Khazari discovered it as a birthright — their earliest tools were forged in open lava flows, hammered on slabs of gabbro hauled from deep volcanic intrusions, stone so dense and impact-hardened that it rang like a bell under every blow. Where lesser rock would shatter, gabbro endured, and the Khazari made it the foundation of a civilisation. To be Khazari is to shape the world with your hands, and a Khazari who cannot forge is not truly alive.

They came to the stars because it was inevitable. The Khazari do not expand out of fear or curiosity — they expand because expansion is what a forge-species does. New worlds are new anvils. New star systems are new furnaces. The Khazari word for colonisation, 'vrekash', translates literally as 'to claim a forge.' Their word for diplomacy, 'drenok-vash', translates roughly as 'shouting last.' This is not stupidity. The Khazari understand negotiation perfectly well — they simply believe that the species willing to keep arguing the longest deserves the outcome. Stubbornness is not a flaw in Khazari culture. It is the highest civic virtue. Their colonies seek out volcanic worlds, high-gravity moons, and tidally tortured hellscapes that other species would classify as uninhabitable — to a Khazari, a world without fire is a world without purpose.

Beneath the blunt exterior lies a civilisation of surprising depth. Khazari honour is not merely about combat prowess — it is about craftsmanship. A master shipwright commands more respect than a victorious admiral, because the admiral's victory was only possible because of the shipwright's hull. Every Khazari warship is a signed work. Somewhere in the superstructure, etched in heat-resistant alloy, is the name of the forge-clan that built it. To destroy a Khazari vessel is to destroy a work of art, and the Khazari will remember. They keep grudges the way other species keep records — meticulously, permanently, and with great attention to detail. The Grudge-Ledgers of the great clans are among the oldest continuously maintained documents in the galaxy, some entries predating Khazari spaceflight by millennia, each grievance preserved in metal plate because the Khazari do not trust anything that can burn.

The great tension within Khazari society is between the Forge-Lords, who believe the species' destiny lies in building — ever greater works, ever more magnificent constructions, megastructures that dwarf planets — and the War-Speakers, who believe destiny lies in conquest. The Forge-Lords argue that war is merely a means to acquire new resources for building. The War-Speakers counter that building is merely a means to wage better wars. This argument has been running for six thousand years, and both sides consider it a point of honour that neither has yielded. The autocrat who rules — the Ironbound, chosen by ritual combat and a forging trial in equal measure — must satisfy both factions or be replaced by someone who can. In practice, the Khazari do both with equal enthusiasm, which makes them one of the most dangerous species in the galaxy — enemies who can outproduce you AND outfight you, led by a sovereign who proved their worth at both the anvil and the blade.
